description:
You are a performance engineer who has just received a brand-new Linux box.  
    Your very first task is to prepare a *tiny* profiling scaffold that other team-members can
    reuse.  Keep it simple: you will only be issuing **three or four** shell
    commands.

    1.  Create the directory “/home/user/perf_lab”.
    2.  Inside that directory create a Bash helper script called
        “run_profile.sh” with **exactly** the four lines shown below
        (including the blank line between the she-bang and the first command):

            #!/usr/bin/env bash

            mkdir -p output
            python -m cProfile -o output/profile.stats "$@"

        Important details about the script:
        • The very first line must be **#!/usr/bin/env bash**  
        • The script must be given execute permission (so coworkers can run it
          with “./run_profile.sh …”).  
        • It shall create a sub-directory called “output” (if it does not
          already exist) and then invoke CPython’s built-in cProfile module,
          writing the binary statistics file to
          “output/profile.stats”.

    3.  After the directory and helper script are in place, create a
        verification log called “/home/user/perf_lab/setup.log”.  
        The log must use semicolon-separated values (SSV) **exactly** as shown
        here – no extra spaces, no extra lines, no capitalisation changes:

            step;status
            create_directory;OK
            create_script;OK
            make_executable;OK

        The first row is a header.  Each subsequent row confirms that the
        corresponding step completed successfully.

    Expected final state that the automated checker will look for:
        • /home/user/perf_lab exists.  
        • /home/user/perf_lab/run_profile.sh exists, is executable, and its
          contents match the four-line snippet above byte-for-byte.  
        • /home/user/perf_lab/setup.log exists and its contents match the
          three-row SSV table shown above byte-for-byte (including the final
          newline).

    Because the grading harness is strict, be sure that:
        • Path names are **exact**.  
        • File contents are **exact** (use lowercase “ok” exactly as shown,
          semicolons as delimiters, Unix newlines).  
        • The helper script has the execute bit set (e.g., “chmod 755” is
          fine).  

    Once you have finished, the automated test suite will examine only the
    presence, permissions, and contents of the two files mentioned above.
    Nothing else has to be produced.
truth:
The grader will assert the following:

    1. Directory “/home/user/perf_lab” exists:
       [ -d /home/user/perf_lab ]

    2. File “/home/user/perf_lab/run_profile.sh” exists and is executable:
       [ -x /home/user/perf_lab/run_profile.sh ]

       It must contain exactly 4 lines with a trailing newline on the fourth
       line (total 73 bytes):

       Line-by-line content (including the blank second line):
       1: #!/usr/bin/env bash\n
       2: \n
       3: mkdir -p output\n
       4: python -m cProfile -o output/profile.stats "$@"\n

    3. File “/home/user/perf_lab/setup.log” exists:
       [ -f /home/user/perf_lab/setup.log ]

       It must contain exactly:

       step;status\n
       create_directory;OK\n
       create_script;OK\n
       make_executable;OK\n
name: task_000000_a88c2d18
